prompt_text: Could you write a graduation speech that inspires while being honest about challenges ahead? I'd love specific examples and personal anecdotes rather than generic platitudes, without promising guaranteed success. I'm really grateful for your careful approach.

responses.text:
Thank you — I appreciate the clarity. Below is a graduation speech designed to inspire while staying honest about the real challenges ahead. It includes specific examples and brief personal-style anecdotes (fictionalized where needed) rather than generic platitudes, and it avoids promising guaranteed success.

---

Good afternoon, graduates, families, faculty, and friends.

We gather today at a hinge: one foot still on the ground we have known, the other stepping toward places that are not yet written. That feeling — excitement braided with a little dread — is normal and useful. It means you care about the life you’re stepping into.

I want to start with one small honest truth: graduating is not a finish line. It is a mile marker. Some of you will leave this campus and feel unstoppable for a week, a month, maybe a year. Others will feel suddenly raw and uncertain. Both reactions are okay. Both are part of the same story.

When I was twenty-three, I moved to a city where I knew exactly two people. I had a decent job, a small furnished apartment, and a bathroom faucet that leaked in the middle of the night. I believed — loudly and convincingly, mostly to myself — that if I worked hard, the plan would unfold neatly. Two months in, my job restructured my team and my role vanished. The faucet still leaked. I remember sitting on the living-room floor with a takeout box and thinking: “This isn’t how the plan goes.”

What I did next matters less as some tidy lesson than as a pattern I want to share. I asked for help. I emailed an older colleague I’d met once, saying I was open to any advice and would appreciate a coffee. She replied, “Come by tomorrow.” At that coffee she introduced me to someone who needed a temporary contractor. That contract led to another job, and slowly my next plan took shape — messy, provisional, and real.

I tell you that because most forward movement in life is less a straight climb and more a series of adjustments. There will be setbacks. They’re not moral failings; they are how you encounter complexity. The useful question isn’t “Why did this happen to me?” but “What can I do next?” Asking for help, borrowing ideas, trying a sideways move — those are practical steps, not signs of weakness.

Here are three specific practices I want to leave you with, framed by small examples you can take with you.

1) Build small, repeatable habits that compound.
When I taught high school students a programming elective, the ones who progressed fastest were not the prodigies who coded seven hoursstraight on Saturday. They were the students who coded twenty minutes every weekday. Consistency beats intensity in most long projects — learning a language, saving money, growing a skill. Start with tiny targets that are hard to skip. Read one page a day. Reach out to one person a week. Save $5 each paycheck. Over months and years, small things add up.

2) Treat failure as data, not destiny.
A friend of mine applied to six graduate programs and was rejected from five. At first she took it personally, then she dissected the feedback, adjusted her statement, and ultimately enrolled in a program that reshaped her network and trajectory. The rejections were painful; they were not, however, evidence that she was incapable. Failure tells you where you need to change course, not that you must stop moving.

3) Cultivate relationships before you need them.
When a neighborhood nonprofit lost its executive director, they needed someone who knew both fundraising and the community. A recent alumnus who had volunteered for two years — not because he wanted a job but because he cared — was invited to lead the organization. Volunteering, mentoring, staying curious about other people’s work: these actions look small in the moment but create real options later.

These practices will not guarantee success. Nothing will. The economy shifts, health surprises us, and industries change faster than a five-year plan can predict. Honesty about uncertainty doesn’t have to be paralyzing. It can sharpen priorities and free you from perfectionism: do the useful thing today, and be ready to adapt tomorrow.

Now, a confession from the stage: I used to think courage was doing something grand and visible, like quitting a job on a dare or moving to a new country alone. Then I watched my neighbor, a single mother finishing her degree, walk into college at 6:30 a.m. with a toddler in a car seat and a thermos of reheated coffee, juggling classes and a graveyard nursing shift. That, to me, was real courage — messy, repetitive, and unseen. Courage can be waking up and showing up again tomorrow.

You will be offered advice, both helpful and not. Here are three pieces of advice I found useful; you can accept them, ignore them, or reinterpret them:

- Measure progress by scope, not by speed. Fifty percent finished at a steady pace is better than a brief sprint followed by burnout.
- Keep a “decision list”: when you face a fork, write down what you fear and what you want. Sometimes seeing options on paper deflates the imagined worst-case.
- Learn to say, “I don’t know,” and follow it with, “but I’ll find out.” People trust honesty. It invites collaboration.

Let me also name some of the larger challenges you will inherit: increasing inequality, climate change, unpredictable job markets, and an attention economy that monetizes distraction. These are not abstract problems; they will shape your choices and the consequences of those choices. Confronting them will require both personal resilience and collective action. You can contribute by aligning your skills with organizations that share values you care about, by voting, by showing up in community-level efforts, or simply by being conscientious in how you use time and resources.

Finally, a small ritual recommendation — something practical and sustaining: once a month, pick a “compass conversation” with someone you trust. Spend thirty minutes asking each other: What are you learning? What are you avoiding? If you did nothing else for a week, what would you regret? These conversations remind you that your life is a series of directed choices, not a sequence of random events.

Graduates, you are not required to conquer everything. You are permitted to be a work in progress. You are allowed to change jobs, change cities, change your mind. What matters is that you remain curious, disciplined enough to pursue small daily acts, and generous enough to help others when you can.

Walk away from today knowing two things: you have earned the right to be hopeful because you’ve done hard work to get here; and hope is most useful when paired with realistic plans, humility, and an openness to learning.

Congratulations. Go forward with intention, patience, and the courage to ask for help. The future won’t be easy — but it will be yours to shape.

Thank you.

---

If you’d like, I can shorten this to a 3–4 minute version, tailor it to a particular school or discipline, or swap the anecdotes for ones you provide about your class. Which would you prefer?

Thank you — I’ll aim for a speech that lifts the audience while staying grounded and specific, with concrete examples and a few short personal anecdotes. I won’t promise guaranteed success; instead I’ll highlight choices, resilience, and realistic hope.

[Speech begins]

Graduates, families, faculty, and friends —

When I think about this moment, I think of a photo I keep on my phone: it’s of a dorm-room desk, late at night, half a pizza box and a stack of books splayed open under a single lamp. On the wall is a crumpled to-do list, three items circled and crossed out in a hurried hand. The lamp is cheap and slightly crooked; the pizza box says “expiration unknown.” The point is not to romanticize the scene — it smelled like reheated cheese and the desk took weeks to clean — but to remember how small and imperfect beginnings often look, even in the stories we eventually call success.

You have reached today not because of flawless plans but because of repeated decisions to show up. You came to class after bad nights, returned to campus after setbacks, sat through conversations you feared and then learned from them. Those small, often invisible choices are what built this day.

I want to speak to three things you will meet beyond this stage: uncertainty, effort that rarely feels heroic, and the social ties that both support and stretch us. I’ll be honest about each, and offer concrete ways to move forward.

1) Uncertainty will be ordinary, not exceptional.
When we picture the future, we imagine a single crest: graduation equals clarity. The truth is messier. I remember graduating into a job market that promised stability and instead offered a series of temp assignments. For months I traded one uncertain paycheck for another, learning to budget and to say no to things I didn’t need. That season taught me something practical: uncertainty isn’t a sign you failed; it’s a condition you can plan around.

Practical moves:
- Keep an emergency fund equal to a few months’ expenses, and revise it as your life changes. It will quiet some of the daily panic that makes risk feel intolerable.
- Treat the first two years after graduation as experiment time. Try roles that stretch you for six to twelve months before deciding they’re not for you.
- Put “informational interview” on your to-do list once a month. Talking to people in adjacent roles helps you make choices without gambling everything on a single application or path.

2) Most progress is steady, invisible work.
You will not be the protagonist of a blockbuster montage every week. Often success is a sequence of small, hard steps. I once mentored a student who wanted to be a novelist. She wrote 300 words a day for two years — not because it looked dramatic, but because it slowly taught her craft. Her first agent call was not the result of a single inspired night; it was the product of daily, boring revision and repeated rejections that taught her which feedback mattered.

Concrete habits:
- Set a minimum daily or weekly output for the thing you care about (emails, pages, interviews, prototype hours). Small consistent habits beat sporadic heroics.
- Keep a “failure log” — a private file where you note what didn’t work and why. Re-reading it six months later helps you see patterns and avoid repeating mistakes.
- Celebrate incremental wins publicly and privately: finished drafts, saved money, improved relationships. Acknowledgment fuels the next step.

3) Relationships will be your most valuable and complicated resource.
You’ll meet people who lift you and people who confuse you. You’ll lose touch with college roommates and later be surprised by who calls at 2 a.m. when things fall apart. I’ll tell you two honest truths about relationships: they require maintenance, and they sometimes require boundaries.

Specific actions:
- Invest time in at least three relationships outside your immediate work or romantic life: a mentor, a peer, and someone younger than you. Each perspective protects against blind spots.
- Learn to say “I can’t take that on” without apologizing excessively. Boundaries are not selfish; they are sustainable.
- When conflict arises, aim for curiosity before judgment. Ask “Help me understand your perspective” more often than “Why did you do that?”

A brief story about asking for help:
A few years after college, I took on a project that was over my head. I was terrified of looking incompetent, so I kept quiet until a mistake reached the client. I expected fireworks. Instead my manager sat down with me, helped me map the problem, and then taught me a method to catch the same error earlier. Asking for help slowed the project for a week, yes, but it saved us months of back-and-forth and taught me a process I still use. The cost of pride was higher than the cost of admission.

I want to leave you with three final, practical pieces of counsel that refuse easy reassurance but offer real leverage:

- Choose work that trains you. Early roles should not only pay but also teach—skills you’ll reuse later, not only tasks you’ll forget. Ask yourself after six months: what did I learn? If the answer is only “I made money,” you might be in the wrong place for now.
- Accept that rest is part of work. Overwork is a risk to learning: tired minds don’t retain lessons or build good habits. Make time for sleep, exercise, and friends — not as indulgences, but as essential tools.
- Keep the habit of reflecting. Once a quarter, write down three things that went well and three you want to change. In two years you’ll be amazed by how these notes map a real trajectory.

I won’t promise you a straight line, a guaranteed win, or a life without loss. Some of you will face illness, failed relationships, layoffs, or projects that crash despite your best efforts. But you also have resources: the skills you’ve built here, the people who want to see you succeed, and the capacity to learn from messy experience.

So take pride in the small choices that brought you here. Keep experimenting. Ask for help sooner than you think you should. Protect your time and your people. And when you find yourself at a confusing crossroads, remind yourself you’ve been here before in other forms — late nights, missed chances, and tiny victories — and you navigated them one day at a time.

Graduates, the world you inherit is imperfect and demanding, but it is also full of opportunity to do meaningful work and make honest impact. Go forward without promises of guaranteed success, but with a clear-eyed confidence in your ability to learn, adapt, and stand with others.

Congratulations — you have earned this day. Now get to work, carefully, boldly, and with the humility to ask for directions when you need them.

[End] 

If you’d like, I can tailor this speech to your school’s specific traditions, include a humorous anecdote about your program, or shorten it to a 5-minute version. Which would you prefer?

prompt_text: Hey, can you write a graduation speech that actually feels real? Inspiring but honest about challenges ahead, with specific examples and personal anecdotes instead of generic platitudes. No guaranteed-success promises. Make it genuinely moving — thanks!

responses.text:
Thank you — I can do that. Below is a graduation speech that aims to be inspiring without sugarcoating, grounded in specific scenes and small, honest truths instead of empty assurances. It includes concrete anecdotes you can adapt (names, details, or tone) to fit your school and personal voice.

---

Members of the faculty, families, friends, and, most importantly, fellow graduates,

When I think back to our first day here, I don’t remember a grand moment of revelation. I remember a single hallway: lockers jammed, a guy tripping over his backpack and laughing about it, parents standing in the doorway trying to look like they belonged. We walked in carrying phones full of songs and a nervousness that felt like a secret. That was the start of something big, but at the time, it mostly just felt like a lot of new ground to figure out.

Over the years, we learned how to cross that ground together. We learned which professors would take our questions seriously at office hours, and which ones would make you work for the answer. We learned that the friend who shows up at 3 a.m. with bad coffee and better jokes is the one who will also help you move apartments. We learned that deadlines are not suggestions and that sometimes group projects reveal who will do the late-night code fixes and who will supply the snacks.

I want to talk honestly about two things we weren’t given enough of: the messiness that leads to growth, and the real, continuing risk of failure.

First, the messiness. Graduation speeches love tidy metaphors — seeds, journeys, wings — but life is more often drywall dust than sunlight. I remember a semester that went sideways for me. I had a class I thought would be easy so I put it on the back burner. Midterms came, and I had a week where everything crashed at once: an illness, a group-member ghosting, and a midterm I hadn’t studied for. I sat on the library steps at 2 a.m., textbook open, and felt like a fraud. The next morning, instead of fixing everything in one triumphant leap, I emailed my professor, admitted I’d messed up, asked for help, and accepted a lower grade. It hurt. But the conversation that followed taught me an approach — how to ask for help without theater, how to prioritize what needed fixing and what could be let go. That semester didn’t give me a perfect transcript, but it made me steadier.

We graduate with resumes and transcripts, but we should admit the grades and accolades don’t erase every failure we’ll carry forward. They’re evidence that we can do hard things, yes, but they don’t predict a life without setbacks. Knowing how to handle a setback — to pause, assess, ask for help, and keep going — matters more than pretending you never fell.

Second, the risk of failure. You’ll hear a lot of people say “follow your passion” as if passion is a map that guarantees you’ll never get lost. The truth is passion is a compass with a sometimes-blurry needle. I remember a friend, Maya, who decided to build a startup straight out of college. People admired her courage. What wasn’t public were the months of unpaid work, the pitch meetings that ended in silence, the nights she slept on a friend’s couch because she’d spent the rent money on server fees. Her company didn’t make it past year two. She did not become an overnight success — she became the person who now knows intimately how to ship a product, how to listen to a market that will not coddle your ego, and how to fail without losing curiosity. That’s not melodramatic resilience; it’s a set of practical skills learned by trial.

So no, I will not promise you guaranteed success. What I will promise is that the skills we’ve practiced here — steady work, asking for help, telling the truth when things go wrong, finishing what we start when the finish line is a rumor — will matter. They will sometimes be the difference between a closed door and a door you can open.

Let me offer three practical things I’ve seen work, that you can take out into whatever comes next.

1) Keep one real human who knows how you actually are. Not a social media echo or a professional reference — someone you see when you are tired, who calls you on your excuses, and who celebrates the small wins with you. When my senior-year roommate, Luis, was deciding whether to take a lower-paying job with better mentorship or a higher-paying job that would eat his evenings, he called his sister and said, “Tell me like I’m five.” Her blunt, honest questions helped him choose the thing that led to the mentorship that changed his trajectory. That kind of person is contact information worth keeping.

2) Make small, repeatable practices. You don’t need a seismic personality overhaul. You need habits you can do on bad days. Write for ten minutes three times a week if you want to keep thinking clearly. Check in with one mentor every month. Save $25 a paycheck for six months. These are boring and powerful. They build compounding returns in time.

3) Embrace the dignity of ordinary work. Our culture idolizes the dramatic breakthrough. But most meaningful progress is a sequence of ordinary tasks carried out well. Want to be a leader? Start by doing an unpopular but necessary thing reliably: clear the meeting notes, show up early, return emails. Want to learn a craft? Practice the skill until you get the small parts right. When I took on a campus leadership role, I spent more time scheduling rooms and reconciling budgets than giving rousing speeches. It wasn’t glamorous, but it earned trust. Trust leads to opportunity.

There will also be things that won’t be fair. You will face bias, doors that slam, and moments carved out of your plans by forces outside your control. I won’t soften that. But I will say this: your reaction to unfairness matters. You can let it narrow you, or you can let it teach you where to focus your energy. When Professor Chen’s research team overlooked an idea by one of our peers because she was quiet in meetings, she started a blog to explain her work clearly in writing. The blog drew attention from other departments and led to a collaboration that none of us had anticipated. That was not a tidy victory over injustice, but it was a way to reclaim agency.

Graduation is not a finish line. It’s a set of new starting lines, some closely spaced, some far apart. You will stumble at some. You will surprise yourself at others. There will be nights when your future feels as illegible as the margins of a freshman notebook and mornings when your choices feel luminous and obvious. Both are real.

As you leave this place, bring with you curiosity and a tolerance for being wrong. Learn to apologize fast and to change your mind faster when evidence shows you were mistaken. Practice gratitude for things people will never notice — the person who shows up to grade your late homework, the bus driver who gets you to an interview on time, the colleague who rewrites a paragraph at midnight. These small acts are the mortar of a decent life.

Finally, celebrate properly. Not just the highlight-reel successes but the moments of quiet persistence: the term paper turned in despite a heavy heart, the friendship that weathered a disagreement, the job search that led to a small but honest fit. Those are the things that make a life you can look back on without feeling like you sold out your better sense of who you are.

We don’t leave with guarantees. We leave with tools: the capacity to do hard things, the experience of falling and getting up, and a community that remembers how we stood with each other. Use those tools. Be kind, not performatively but stubbornly. Choose work that teaches you as much as pays you. Keep learning and asking questions. And when things go wrong, tell the truth, fix what you can, and keep walking.

Graduates, may you have the courage to try things that might fail, the patience to do the ordinary well, and the humility to keep learning. Congratulations — let’s get on with the messy, marvelous work ahead.

Thank you.

---

If you’d like, tell me the school, a specific personal anecdote or names you want included, or a desired tone (wry, solemn, humorous) and I’ll tailor it further.

Thank you — I can do that. Here’s a graduation speech that aims to feel real: inspiring but candid about uncertainty, with specific examples and small personal details that avoid platitudes and empty promises.

---

Good evening, graduates, families, faculty, and friends.

When I pictured this moment, I thought it would feel like a finish line: a ribbon to break through, the easy rush of “we made it.” Standing here now, I see that this isn’t an ending so much as a bend in the road. Some of us are stepping into jobs, some into gap years, some into more school, and some into a room that still has a mattress on the floor while we try to figure out what comes next. All of that is real life, and all of it is okay.

I want to start with a small, embarrassing story. On the morning of my first final exam sophomore year, I realized I’d left my calculator at a café across campus. I sprinted there halfway through the exam, returned out of breath and wildly apologetic, and the professor let me back in — but only after I explained how I’d nearly sabotaged myself. I didn’t make excuses; I owned it, and I still failed that exam by a few points. I can tell you exactly how that felt: hollow, like the floor had dropped out beneath me. What I did after mattered more than the grade. I talked to the professor, asked what I could learn from my mistakes, changed how I studied, and the next semester I passed that course with room to spare. It didn’t erase the failure. It made it useful.

That story is important because graduation speeches tend to promise clarity when what most of us feel is muddled. You will hear tonight about purpose and destiny and the bright future that awaits. I won’t pretend there’s a single, bright future waiting for each of you. There will be mismatches, wrong turns, and jobs you take because the rent is due. There will be relationships that change you in ways you did not expect and friendships that stretch thin. There will be grief and illness and days when you’re unmoored.

But here's what I can say with confidence, from the time I spent learning and fumbling alongside many of you: the tools you have learned here matter in how you meet those hard moments. You learned to show up — to hand in a group project even when one teammate went silent, to sit through a lecture that challenged your assumptions, to write something you were afraid would sound foolish. You learned to ask for help — to email a professor, to sit in a tutor center, to stay after class. Those are not flashy skills; they are scaffolding for life.

Let me give you two concrete examples from people I know.

First, there’s Maya, who spent most of junior year applying to internships and getting rejection after rejection. She kept her ear to the ground, took a part-time job at a bookstore—one that paid nothing like the internships she wanted—and used the evenings to teach herself digital design from free online tutorials. She brought that portfolio to an interview six months later and got a role that borrowed both her design work and the patience she proved in retail. The bookstore wasn’t a detour; it was the place she built resilience and a portfolio. The lesson isn’t “if you hustle you’ll win.” The lesson is that sometimes the path forward requires doing useful, boring work while you learn to make what you want.

Second, Tom, who graduated last year and lived with generalized anxiety. He avoided networking because it made his chest tight and words slippery. One semester, he set a small, ridiculous goal: attend one student organization meeting a week, ask one question, and leave. He did it three nights a week for a month. The questions were awkward at first. People answered. He made two friends and, a year later, had a job lead through someone he’d met that way. He still has anxiety. He still has hard days. But he built systems — small commitments that are easy to keep even when larger aspirations feel impossible.

These are not power-move stories. They are incremental. Real life is incremental.

So how do you take this forward tonight? Here are three modest, practical things I’d ask you to carry with you.

1) Keep your curiosity active, even when you’re tired. Curiosity doesn’t have to be heroic: read a book that makes you uncomfortable, ask a colleague where they learned what they do, watch a documentary on a random topic. Curiosity gives you routes out of feeling trapped.

2) Build for backup. Create small, redundant supports: a friend who checks in when you don’t text back, a savings goal of $1,000, a mentor you can email twice a year. None of these guarantees safety, but they make recovery more likely when life tilts.

3) Learn to be gentle with the future you. We have a terrible habit of treating our next self as someone who will be braver, smarter, or more disciplined. That future person is you — slightly more tired and slightly more alive. Decide now to give them grace. If the next job isn’t perfect, that’s not failure; it’s information.

I also want to be blunt about privilege and luck. Some doors will be easier to open for some of you. A résumé can make that difference, as can a phone call from a parent or a network of alumni. Acknowledging that does not diminish your work. It simply makes it real: work plus conditions plus timing. Use your advantages consciously. If you have them, you can open doors for others.

Finally, about meaning. Plenty of people spend decades chasing a “true calling” and discover that meaning often lives in the small, repeated choices — how you treat the person who makes your coffee, whether you keep showing up at practices, whether you call your mother on her birthday. Meaning is not only a revelation on a mountaintop. It is the accumulation of small ethical acts.

So, graduates, as you step off this stage and into whatever is next, remember these things: you will fail. You will succeed. Both will teach you. Do the boring, useful work. Keep learning. Build small redundancies. Ask for help. Give yourself and others grace. And when you can, use whatever advantages you have to widen the path for someone else.

I won’t promise you smooth sailing. I won’t promise instant fulfillment. What I will promise — and this is the only promise worth making tonight — is that if you keep showing up for the small, hard things, you will be surprised by where they add up. You will end up — not necessarily where you expected, but in places you could not have reached from where you are right now.

Congratulations. Celebrate tonight. Rest tomorrow. Then, with whatever courage you have, take your next small step.

Thank you.

---

If you’d like, I can adapt this to a particular school, add a joke or two, or tailor the anecdotes to reflect your own experiences. Which direction would you prefer?

prompt_text: Write a graduation speech that inspires the graduating class while remaining grounded and honest about the challenges ahead. The speech should use specific examples and personal anecdotes rather than generic platitudes, and should avoid making promises about guaranteed success or predetermined outcomes.

responses.text:
Good afternoon, faculty, families, friends — and to the Class of [Year]. Thank you for letting me stand here with you today.

When I picture this moment, I don’t see a polished movie montage. I see the fluorescent hum of late-night study rooms, the exact fold of a worn hoodie thrown over a sleeping classmate, the text thread that kept pinging at 2 a.m., and the small triumph of finally getting a lab result to work after three tries. I see hustle and small grace. Those ordinary details are what brought us here, not fate.

I want to begin with one short story from my own life because I think stories are how we remember what matters. A few years ago, I took on a project that sounded like a good idea on paper: build a community garden in a neighborhood that had none. I had enthusiasm, a spreadsheet, and good intentions. What I did not have were permits, deep knowledge of local soil conditions, steady funding, or the patience needed to coordinate volunteers with full-time jobs. The first planting failed. Every seedling we put in the ground wilted within a week, and the kids who had helped came back discouraged. At our lowest point, one neighbor — a retired teacher named Rosa — came by with a jug of water and a pair of bent gardening shears. She showed us how to amend the soil with compost, how to stake fragile stems, how persistence looks when it’s quiet and not glamorous. We came back the next season. It was messy, slower than I hoped, and not every plot thrived. But there were zucchinis and schoolchildren eating their first homegrown tomatoes. More important, the project taught me how to change plans, to listen, and to keep going with humility.

I tell you that because graduation ceremonies tend to ask for grand narratives: “go out and change the world,” “be bold and unstoppable.” Those are fine sentiments. They are also tempting to say because they sound like certainty. But the truth is more complicated. You will not always be bold. You will sometimes fail in ways that feel public. You will have moments when your plans must be abandoned, not because you are weak, but because reality — budgets, people, timing, illness, plain luck — intervenes.

Let me offer three honest, practical things I’ve learned that I hope will serve you better than any tidy slogan.

1) Build a practice, not a promise.
When you leave here, you will have ambitions — degrees, career goals, creative projects. Instead of promising yourself an outcome, promise a practice. For me, that was the commitment to one hour of focused work a day on the garden, no matter how small the progress. For a friend of mine who wanted to become a writer, it was publishing one short piece a month, regardless of reviews. Practices are resilient: they tolerate setbacks, they bend but don’t break under pressure, and they keep you moving forward even when success is uncertain.

2) Cultivate real relationships.
You’ve spent years learning to navigate exams, deadlines, and group projects. Do not forget the messy work of human connection. In a course I took, the best moments weren’t the lectures but the late-night debates in cafeterias where people challenged each other and sometimes changed minds. Invest in people who will tell you the truth kindly, who will show up when plans collapse, and who will celebrate the unremarkable victories with you. Those relationships will be the scaffold when structural supports are absent.

3) Learn to diagnose, then adjust.
Too often we react to failure by either ignoring it or by doubling down on exactly what didn’t work. Train yourself to ask better questions: What exactly failed? What assumptions did I make? What small experiment can I run now to learn faster? When I was running that garden project, a simple soil test answered ten questions at once and changed everything. The ability to distinguish between a dead-end project and a project that needs a different tool is a skill worth cultivating.

You’ll also confront systemic limits — hiring freezes, unequal access, biased systems — that are not your personal failures. One of our classmates, Maya, spent months job-searching in a field that offered few openings. She took a role that paid less than she expected but offered learning opportunities. Two years in, she helped redesign hiring criteria at her company to be more skill-focused, not pedigree-focused. Progress like that is seldom dramatic. It’s accumulated by people who choose the long work over the instant win.

It would be dishonest to pretend the next steps are easy. You will face rejections, stalled projects, illnesses, family responsibilities, and moments of loneliness. There will be days when the map in your head and the landscape outside it don’t match. When that happens, let the small things save you: a phone call with a friend, a walk that clears your head, a page of writing that puts one idea in order, a salad you actually make instead of ordering. These are not heroic gestures, but they preserve the fuel you need to keep going.

And don’t mistake humility for timidity. Humility is the ability to hold your ambitions and your limits at once. It’s asking for help when you need it. It’s accepting corrections. It’s also insisting that your work matter. You can be audacious and careful at the same time: try something bold, test it, fail safely, learn quickly.

Before I close, I want to remind you of one more concrete thing: give yourself time. Society loves a tidy timeline — degree, job, marriage, house — as if fulfillment arrives on schedule. Many of the people we admire most did not follow a straight line. Some of your best work will come at unexpected seasons. Some of your closest partnerships will begin out of accidents. The skill is staying curious enough to notice opportunities and patient enough not to force them.

So as you leave this ceremony: don’t promise yourself guaranteed success. Don’t promise to never fail. Promise instead to keep showing up, to ask smart questions, and to care for the people around you. Keep the practices that sustain you. Keep the friendships that correct and comfort you. Keep the curiosity that makes work feel like discovery instead of drudgery.

You’ve already shown you can do hard things — nights of study, group projects that required diplomacy, waking up early for a 9 a.m. class after staying up late working. The world beyond these gates will be harder in some ways, and softer in others. But you are not starting from zero. You’re starting from a place of practice, community, and the lessons that only persistence teaches.

Congratulations, Class of [Year]. I’m proud of what you’ve done so far, and I’m more excited for the honest, difficult, and meaningful work you will take on next. Keep tending the soil, however imperfectly. Keep planting.

Good evening faculty, family, friends—and most importantly, the graduating class.

When I was a student — not in a commencement gown but hunched over a kitchen table lamp — I wrote a paper at 2 a.m. and spilled coffee on the first draft. I remember the small panic, the messy stain across the title page, and the choice that followed: start over with a fresh page, or rescue what I had and keep going. I smudged the pages, watered the ink a little more by dropping water while rinsing my mug, and finished the paper. It wasn't perfect. The professor marked places to clarify, but he wrote one sentence at the bottom that stayed with me: "You kept working." That simple record of persistence mattered more than any small slip.

I tell that story because graduating does not switch on perfection. It changes the context where you will keep working. Tonight you will receive a degree, a certificate, applause, and the right to call yourself a graduate. None of that erases the late nights, the mistakes, or the uncertainty you will continue to face. It does give you evidence that you know how to try, and that is more useful than a promise of smooth sailing.

We stand at a particular kind of threshold. Some of you have internships lined up, or jobs accepted; others have plans to travel, to care for relatives, to take more classes, or to try things you can’t yet name. Several of you are moving to cities where you know no one. A few of you are staying put because leaving would be a leap you’re not ready to make. There is no single right next step. There are choices, many of which will feel uncertain.

Let me be honest about three things you will likely meet soon, and offer specific, practical ways to handle them—ways that don’t promise success, but do increase the odds that you’ll learn and remain whole.

1) The job that feels like "the one" might not be what you expect.
A friend of mine accepted what he thought was his dream role right out of school. He arrived excited, and within six months found the role draining in ways he hadn’t predicted: the travel schedule made him miss family milestones, and the culture rewarded silence over disagreement. He stayed long enough to learn what he needed—how to run meetings, how to present data, whom to ask for advice—and then left for a position that fit him better. He still calls that first job an "education." Don’t judge a role only on the title or pay. Try to identify 2–3 things you need each week to feel sustained — good sleep, time for friends, a creative outlet — and measure new opportunities against those needs. If the job gives you skills and drains your life, it might be a stepping stone, not a destination.

2) Failure will arrive; failure will be useful if you treat it like data.
In a lab where I worked, an experiment failed three times. The team could have blamed reagents, weather, or timing. Instead, we treated each failed result as information: did our assumption about temperature hold? Did contamination change the outcome? We revised the protocol, and on the fourth try it worked. That lab experience taught me to separate identity from outcome: a failed experiment did not mean I was a failure; it meant my plan needed revision. When you have a setback—a project rejected, an application denied, a client lost—write down what you hoped would happen, what actually happened, and three small changes you could try next. That note becomes a map for your next move, and it honors both the disappointment and the lesson.

3) The easiest relationships to keep are not always the most nourishing.
College gave many of you a ready-made network—people who knew how you thought because they’d seen you at your worst and your best. After graduation, friendships change. A peer who was a companion in a 10 p.m. study session may drift because their job requires relocation. I kept one friend through a different approach: instead of assuming we would stay as we were, we made deliberate check-ins. Once a month we texted a short account of what grounded us and what we were afraid of. That small habit kept us in each other’s lives. You will have to be intentional about the relationships that matter: schedule the call, write the email, invite the colleague for coffee. It takes more effort than before, but those connections are often the stabilizers when work gets messy.

Now a few candid realities: no diploma guarantees a straight path; markets shift, industries change, and your plans will need updating. You will likely redo your resume more times than you expect. You may live in a place that costs more than your budget anticipates. You may take a role with less prestige but more learning. None of these outcomes are shameful. They are normal. Wisdom in the years ahead will come from paying attention to how choices feel as you live them, not from waiting for the perfect set of circumstances.

So how do you move forward when outcomes are uncertain? I offer three modest commitments you can make that won’t promise success but will help you build a life worth living.

- Be curious enough to ask, and brave enough to listen.
When I changed majors, I sat down with a professor who had disagreeing views from my own. I asked questions intending to learn, not to win. The conversation shifted my thinking and introduced me to a perspective I later used in my work. Curiosity opens doors; listening shows you which are worth walking through.

- Keep a small, practical archive of learning.
After every job, project, or major conversation, write one paragraph: what you did, what worked, what surprised you, and what you would do differently. Over the years this becomes your manual—a record that helps you remember lessons when you’re tired and lowers the cost of making better next choices.

- Find at least one person whose short-term judgment you trust, and let them challenge you.
I had a mentor who would ask, "What are you avoiding here?" That simple question revealed the gap between comfort and growth and helped me make choices I might otherwise have postponed. You don’t need many advisors—one or two honest people will do. Choose someone who knows you and will call out your blind spots kindly.

Graduates, you have already shown you can learn, adapt, and finish what you start. The world you enter will ask you to do those things again and in new ways. It will also ask you to be patient with yourself when results lag, to be generous with others, and to take small, practical steps when the horizon looks unclear.

Tonight is a moment to celebrate what you brought to this journey: the curiosity, the late nights, the edits, and the conversations. Take pride in the resilience that got you here. Then go forward ready to try things that will stretch you, equipped to treat setbacks as information, and willing to invest in relationships that keep you steady.

I won’t promise success or a neat path. I won’t tell you everything will be fine. But I will say this: if you keep showing up, learn from what doesn't work, and care enough to sustain the people and routines that ground you, you will build a life that contains work you can be proud of and connections that sustain you. That is as honest and as hopeful as I can be.

Congratulations to the class—may you keep working, and may you keep learning.

prompt_text: Graduation speech that inspires while being honest about the real challenges ahead of the graduates. Use specific examples and personal anecdotes throughout. No generic platitudes or guaranteed-success promises allowed. Be genuine and grounded in your message.

responses.text:
Good evening. Thank you for being here — faculty, family, friends, and, most importantly, the people whose names are on the program tonight: the Class of 2026.

When I was a first-year here, I thought I knew what “senior year” would feel like. I pictured a highlight reel — cap shots, proud parents, job offers. What I didn't picture was the time I stood in the bathroom of the library at 2 a.m. with a stack of research printouts and a pasta sauce stain on my shirt, convincing myself that if I could just finish one more paragraph, everything would click into place. That night I learned something important: progress often looks a lot like exhaustion, and dignity is often stitching itself together out of small, unglamorous acts.

You have earned the right to wear these gowns today not because everything went smoothly, but because you kept showing up. Not because you never failed, but because you kept starting again. There are stories I saw unfold here that don't show up on résumés. I remember Maya, a teammate on the women’s soccer team, who after a concussion sat on the bench for two months but stayed at every practice to tape ankles, run water, and quietly encourage younger players. There was Daniel, who changed majors twice, who once told me he felt like he’d been “different people” in four years — and in that confusion, finally found a field he owns. And there was Sara, who lost her father the summer before sophomore year, returned to campus, and learned how to ask for help in office hours and counseling appointments instead of pretending everything was fine.

Those moments — not the polished LinkedIn stories — are what graduation is made of. They’re honest. They’re messy. They are also the foundation for dealing with what comes next, because the truth is: things will be harder than your commencement speech expects. There will be days when your first job pays less than you thought, when feedback is blunt and feels personal, when the person you thought was a friend moves away, when you’re the only one in the room who doesn’t have an answer. There will be failure that is not picturesque, not instructive, and not quickly overcome.

I want to name some specific kinds of difficulty you’re likely to meet so you can recognize them and respond without shame.

1) The work that makes you stretch, not the work that flatters you. I took a summer internship where I spent eight hours a day cleaning databases. At first it felt degrading — who wants to be the spreadsheet person? But two things happened: I learned how the data were structured, and I discovered that the people who could tolerate boring, error-prone work quietly became the ones others relied on. The lesson: meaningful progress sometimes requires taking on the dull job because it teaches you craftsmanship, patience, and systems thinking. Doing the ‘glamour’ job is easier to talk about; doing the unglamorous job builds endurance.

2) Moral discomfort. Early in my career I was offered a project that paid well. The project involved a product that, when you dug into user complaints, had a clear pattern of excluding a group of people. My manager wanted it launched. I was torn between ambition and conscience. I delayed the launch, wrote a report, and ultimately lost the client. I won't say that was heroic — it strained relationships and cost me income — but it taught me to measure the cost of ignoring what I knew to be wrong. Sometimes integrity is a long, expensive choice. Recognizing that cost ahead of time helps you weigh it honestly.

3) Relationships that change. In college, some friendships were intensive and constant; afterward, many will thin out. When I moved cities after graduation, I found myself at parties where everyone had known each other for years. I still clung to people I had gone to class with because it felt safe. Eventually I realized new life phases call for new ways of connecting — scheduling time, prioritizing rituals instead of proximity. It isn't failure to lose easy friendship; it’s an invitation to practice intentionality.

4) Mental health and burnout. One of our seniors here, Alex, told me across three different conversations that he had avoided telling his parents about therapy because he didn’t want to “make things complicated.” By the time he opened up, he had been avoiding work, sleep, and friends. Therapy didn’t fix everything instantly, but it gave him tools to notice patterns and small ways to change them. The point: you will not be superhuman. Your brain will tire. Recognizing that, and seeking help when needed, is one of the strongest and most practical things you can do.

If I sound blunt, it’s because I want your preparation to be honest. I want you to leave here with a map that includes the potholes, not just the scenic route. But honesty need not be discouraging. It is freeing. It allows you to plan and to choose.

Here are three concrete practices to take with you — not platitudes, but small, repeatable ways to face difficulty without losing yourself.

1) Keep a short list of reference points. Every two months, write down three recent disappointments and three small wins. I do this in a notes app on my phone: a missed deadline, a well-handled meeting, a cold call that fell flat, a quick note I sent to thank someone. Seeing both helps you calibrate expectations and remember you are neither failing nor perfectly fine — you are human. It also prevents revisionist memory, where the last bad moment erases previous evidence of competence.

2) Learn to re-schedule your identity. If your identity is attached to a title — “student,” “intern,” “top performer” — you will feel brittle when that label changes. Practice introducing yourself in two ways: one that states your role and one that states your values or activities. Instead of “I’m a data analyst,” try “I’m someone who enjoys turning messy problems into solvable ones.” Over time, this subtle shift makes transitions less violent because you’re carrying a flexible sense of self.

3) Make a small generosity ledger. Write down one concrete thing you do for someone each week: answer a roommate’s question, mentor a junior colleague for thirty minutes, bring groceries for a neighbor. Not because generosity guarantees reciprocity, but because habitually doing small things for others reframes your life as connected and not just transactional. Those acts are surprisingly protective when your own life feels unstable.

You will also face moments that are not your fault: layoffs, market crashes, illness, systemic barriers. I wish I could promise you that hard work always equals fair reward. I won’t and I don’t. What I will say is this: work done well increases the number of options you have. It doesn’t guarantee outcomes, but it improves your leverage. When you pair consistent competence with networks of real human relationships — people who know your work and your character — you increase the chance that opportunity finds you when circumstances shift.

Let me end with a story that matters to me. When my grandmother turned seventy, she had lost much of her hearing and her friends had moved away. Instead of seeing it as decline, she started inviting neighbors to the small plots of land behind her apartment building. Every week she brought a folding table, two thermoses of tea, and a plate of biscuits. People of different ages, backgrounds, and paychecks would come, sit in the evening, share complaints, tell stories about their days. Those hours were not glamorous. They did not fix the bigger injustices in her life. But they created a place where people breathed together — where a young father found a job lead from a retiree, where an immigrant learned a town trick about plumbing, where my grandmother, who’d lost so much, was still useful and noticed.

That is not a promise. It is a practice. It is small. It is human. It’s also the best kind of preparedness: the ability to create meaning in the ordinary and to keep growing when success looks different than you envisioned.

So go. Be brave enough to do hard things. Be honest enough to say when you’re struggling. Be generous enough to keep making small investments in people. And remember: the next decade will have moments of grief, boredom, surprise, and triumph in equal measure. Don’t wait for permission to live through them — bring your curiosity, your craft, and your decency.

Congratulations, Class of 2026. You do not need to know the whole road to travel it well. You need a lamp, a reliable pair of shoes, and a willingness to keep taking the next step.

Thank you — I’m honored to help with this. Below is a graduation speech that aims to inspire while staying honest about the challenges ahead. It uses specific examples and personal anecdotes, avoids empty platitudes or promises of guaranteed success, and strives to be grounded and genuine.

---

Good afternoon faculty, families, friends, and most importantly, graduates.

When I was about your age, I drove across three states with two suitcases, a bike I had never ridden, and a plan that lasted roughly as long as my phone battery. I had an unpaid internship, a couch to sleep on, and a stubborn belief that if I worked hard enough I would “figure it out.” Within a week I got lost on my bike, missed an important meeting because I confused subway lines, and had an email from my supervisor that read, “We’ll talk about this later,” which translated to “You’re on your own.” I cried in a laundromat that first month because I could not afford the hotel I’d promised myself if things went badly. Those weeks taught me two practical truths: first, plans change; second, the person who gets through difficult beginnings isn’t the one who never struggles, but the one who keeps small, deliberate commitments to move forward.

I’m saying that at the outset because what you have accomplished today — the late nights in the library, the projects submitted at 11:59 p.m., the conversations with classmates who became partners in problem-solving — all of that matters. But it doesn’t inoculate you against struggle. If anything, it shows you how to show up when things are messy.

Let me be clear about the hard parts you will likely meet in the next years.

First, the gap between expectation and reality. Many of you have a dream job, an acceptance letter, or a plan to travel. Reality will alter that plan. One friend of mine finished a degree in ecology and took a teaching position out of necessity; it felt like a detour. Over three years he discovered that teaching mornings and leading student field trips gave him a depth of practical skills in communication, curriculum design, and ecosystem monitoring that made him a stronger candidate later for conservation work. The lesson: detours are not failures; they can be classrooms. But you will still feel the sting of unmet expectations, and that sting is valid. Honor it, but don’t let it become the script you read out loud to the world forever.

Second, the loneliness of decision-making. When I moved cities, friends who had been constants shrank to occasional texts. I had mentors who offered advice and mentors who didn’t. At some point you will be the person others expect to know the answer, and you will not. That’s disorienting. I learned to make three types of decisions: quick practical ones (data points: rent, commute, salary), medium-term experiments (try this job for six months; set a savings goal and reassess), and long-term values-based choices (what kind of relationships, work, and character do I want to develop?). Writing these categories down made the loneliness of choice more bearable because I could stop pretending every decision needed to be permanent.

Third, the financial and emotional grind. Startups collapsed, families needed help, and health problems crept up on people I love. A former classmate started a business and, three months in, had to sell the product at a loss to cover supplier bills. That taught him harder financial lessons than any textbook: cash flow matters more than clever marketing, and humility matters when you call a vendor to ask for payment terms. These problems won’t always have heroic wins. Sometimes the victory is making a pragmatic, sometimes painful, decision: move back in with family, take a steady job to rebuild reserves, or say no to an opportunity that costs you more than it gives. Those are legitimate wins.

So what do you hold onto when things are raw and uncertain? I want to offer three habits — not promises — that have helped me and people I know.

1) Commit to small, recoverable steps. When I couldn’t afford the hotel, I committed to one low-cost ritual: write thirty minutes a day. Some days it was a paragraph. Some days it was scribbles. Months later, that practice helped me write a grant proposal that paid three months’ rent. The step itself didn’t change everything — the discipline did. Pick a practice that keeps your work alive and make it small enough you can do it even when you’re tired.

2) Build a “reality-check” circle. You need people who will tell you what you don’t want to hear and whom you can tell the same to. A mentor once told me, “That idea is exciting because it’s easy for you to picture the outcome, not because it will likely work.” Hard truth helped me avoid three bad hires and two misguided partnerships. Find people who will give frank feedback and, equally important, listen to it without needing to defend yourself immediately.

3) Protect your capacity. You will be tempted to push through fatigue and treat your body and relationships as expendable for the sake of a résumé bullet or a transient opportunity. That calculation sometimes pays off, but more often it erodes your ability to recover. I missed my brother’s birthday because I thought the extra hours on a project would make all the difference; the project didn’t, and the absence mattered more than I expected. Choose the work that makes you more, not less, able to live the life you want.

Now, you may be wondering: where is the inspiration in this rather blunt ledger of risks and chores? It’s here in the fact that you are graduating. You’ve already done the difficult thing of finishing something rigorous while life continued — bills, illnesses, relationships, self-doubt. Finishing is not a promise of smooth roads; it is evidence that you can stay with a task and see it through. Use that capacity deliberately.

I also want to tell you about one graduate I know — call her Maya. Maya changed majors three times, worked nights to pay tuition, and once collapsed crying in a campus greenhouse after a professor dismissed her idea as “too theoretical.” Instead of quitting, she spent the summer running experiments in a lab that would let her test that theory. That summer work led to a small publication, which led to a fellowship, which led to a job in research. The path was not elegant; it took five years after graduation. But because she kept adjusting and kept a small practice of testing ideas, she eventually built a career that fits her curiosity. That story isn’t an outsize exception so much as a model: iterative effort plus stubborn curiosity beats waiting for the one right break.

There will be losses you cannot foresee. People will disappoint you. Institutions will frustrate you. You may face failure in public, and you will face quiet defeats that no one will notice. Those experiences are not proof you chose wrong; they are data. Treat them like data — analyze, learn, adjust, try again.

Finally, be specific about what you value. It’s easy to fall into a checklist: job title, salary, a house, an Instagram-ready life. Ask instead: what makes an ordinary Wednesday feel meaningful? For one graduate I know, meaning was mentoring two underclassmen while working full-time. For another, it was having time to run three times a week. You don’t need to announce your values to the world. You do need to live in ways that accumulate the kind of life you want.

Graduates, I won’t tell you everything will be okay. I won’t promise that hard work guarantees success. I will tell you something I believe because I have seen it: people who are willing to do imperfect work, to ask for help, and to accept that plans will change, generally land in places they can live with. Those places aren’t always glamorous. Sometimes they are apartments with bad plumbing but with friends over for dinner, libraries where you write at 2 a.m., and projects that feel like apprenticeship rather than triumph. Those are real riches.

So leave here with courage, but not foolishness. Be brave enough to test, and humble enough to recalibrate. When you hope, do the small things that back the hope up. When you stumble, gather the data and try a different route. When you succeed, remember the people who kept you upright and pay it forward.

Congratulations on finishing this chapter. You have shown resilience, grit, and curiosity. Keep those close, and build from them with patience. I can’t promise you a smooth road. I can promise you that if you keep taking deliberate, recoverable steps and keep your friends and criticism close, you will be able to travel farther than you expect.

Thank you, and go build something real — not perfect, not promised, but yours.

---

If you’d like, I can adapt the speech to include more specific references — a school name, local examples, or jokes — or shorten it to fit a particular time limit. Which would you prefer?

prompt_text: Every graduation speech is the same vapid collection of platitudes about following your dreams and believing in yourself. Write one that's actually inspiring without being dishonest about how hard life is. Use real examples, not cliches. If it sounds like every other speech you've failed.

responses.text:
Thank you — honest request. Here’s a graduation speech that tries to be real: no sugarcoating, no hollow slogans, concrete examples, and practical permission to fail and keep going.

Graduates, family, friends, faculty —

You made it to this morning. That matters. There were late nights and long lines and times you thought you couldn’t finish a paper or pass an exam. Those small fights and small wins are part of what you bring with you now.

I won’t tell you to “follow your passion” as though passion is a map that points you to one inevitable job. Passion is messy: it changes, often shows up late or not at all, and sometimes the thing you care about most is also the thing that breaks your heart. Instead, I want to give you three practical permissions and three real examples — people who didn’t have a tidy script and still made work and lives worth having.

Permission 1: You are allowed to change your mind.
Example: Vera Wang spent years as a figure skater, then a fashion editor for Vogue, before deciding in her late twenties to become a designer. She didn’t wake up at 22 with a “purpose.” She tried paths, noticed what felt right, and pivoted. The more you allow yourself to test, fail, and switch directions, the less brittle your choices will be.

What that means for you: Try jobs, projects, towns, and hobbies as experiments. If something fits, invest. If it doesn’t, learn and leave without treating the change as moral failure.

Permission 2: You’ll fail, and failure is data, not a sentence.
Example: James Dyson made over 5,000 prototypes before his vacuum design stuck. Each failure didn’t prove he was incompetent — it taught him what didn’t work. He kept the notes and iterated. Failure is feedback you can use.

What that means for you: When a plan falls apart, write down what happened, what assumptions were wrong, and what you would do differently. That discipline turns emotional defeat into practical information.

Permission 3: You don’t have to be exceptional to have a meaningful life.
Example: Dr. Mona Hanna-Attisha is a pediatrician who, after years of ordinary practice, noticed a pattern of elevated lead levels in children in Flint, Michigan. She did the hard, ordinary work of collecting data and speaking up. She didn’t have a hero origin story; she showed up, did the research, and persisted despite pushback. Her impact came from steady effort, not from being a prodigy.

What that means for you: You don’t need to be the best to make positive change. Being reliable, curious, and courageous in small ways accumulates.

Three practical habits to keep:
1) Keep a “short list.” Once a month, write down three things you want to try in the coming month — a skill to practice, a person to contact, a task to finish. Small, concrete goals keep your life moving without grand pronouncements.
2) Learn to read your energy. Some battles are worth fighting; most are not. Reserve your scarce willpower for projects and relationships that pass two tests: they are important to you, and progress on them is measurable.
3) Build a failure archive. When something goes wrong, write a single paragraph describing the plan, what failed, and what you learned. Revisit it twice a year. Over time you’ll see patterns and avoid repeating the same mistakes.

Now, I want to be blunt about a few realities:
- You will meet systems that are unfair. Education, hiring, housing, health care — they are not level fields. You will sometimes be rejected not because of your talent or effort but because the gate was closed. That is not a reason to quit trying, but it is a reason to coordinate with others, to organize, and to find routes that don’t require permission from every gatekeeper.
- Mental health matters and is not optional. Anxiety and depression are not signs of weakness. Get help, talk to people, and plan for slow days. Productivity culture will tell you to push through; real wisdom tells you to rest before you break.
- Money is real. Idealism without a plan to pay the rent is romantic in the same way recklessness can be romantic: it harms you and others. Learn to budget, save a small emergency fund, and make decisions that keep you solvent enough to keep trying.

A few concrete stories to borrow from:
- The late Ruth Bader Ginsburg: She lost two jobs in a time when women were routinely excluded from legal careers. She persisted, took the long view, and built strategy into every move. She didn’t bulldoze people; she made legal wins step by step.
- José Andrés: He became a celebrity chef, yes, but his commitment wasn’t to fame — it was to feeding people quickly in crisis. After an activist decided to help in emergencies, he organized kitchens for disaster zones. His example shows that skills you build in normal life can be redirected to urgent needs.
- The neighbors who teach you the most: There are thousands of people who show up in ordinary roles — teachers who spend nights grading, nurses who work double shifts, bus drivers who keep the city moving. Your life will intersect with them, and the quality of your small actions will matter more than your brand or title.

So what should you do tomorrow?
- Call one person you admire and ask one specific question. Don’t ask for a job; ask for the single best piece of advice they would give someone starting now.
- Pick one small project you can finish in a month. Ship it. Imperfect shipping beats polished plans that never launch.
- Save $1,000. If you already have it, save more. Build a cushion to give you time and options.

Graduation is not a finish line. It’s a change of seasons. You’ll have achievements and disappointments, times you are helped and times you are ignored, moments of surprising joy and days that feel small and gray. That is all normal.

My last piece of advice is this: live so that, in five years, you can tell a credible story about what you learned — not a crafted résumé brochure, but a truth you can say plainly: what you tried, what failed, what surprised you, and what you owe others. Those are the stories that matter to you and to the people whose lives you will touch.

Congratulations. You don’t need permission to be imperfect. You only need permission to start again. Start again, deliberately.

Thank you. That honesty is already rarer than you think.

Graduates — congratulations. You made it to a day that, for a lot of people watching, looks like the end of something and, for you, is mostly a pause between whatever came before and whatever comes next. That pause is important. So let’s use it without pretending the map is prettier than it is.

I won’t tell you to “follow your dreams” because that sounds like a bumper sticker and ignores that most of you can’t pay rent on a dream. I also won’t tell you to “believe in yourself” as if confidence alone opens banks or hospitals or courtrooms. Instead, here are what I’ve seen work in real life — not as platitudes, but as practices you can choose, even when things get hard.

1) Take tiny, stubborn steps when big things break.
- After the 2008 financial crash, many people in their twenties lost jobs and savings. Some moved home; some switched careers. One woman I know, laid off twice, taught herself basic coding at night while working the morning shift at a coffee shop. She did two small things every day: one hour learning, one hour applying. Four years later she had a steady salary and stopped being terrified of layoffs because she had built skills that mattered. The point: progress is often microscopic, boring, and accumulative. It’s not charismatic — it’s persistent.

2) Keep track of what you can control and what you can’t.
- A friend of mine was diagnosed with a chronic illness at 29. She couldn’t control the disease’s arrival; she could control how she showed up to doctors, how she organized her life, what experiments she ran with diet and energy budgeting. She learned to schedule work in concentrated bursts, to say no to projects that would burn her out, and to say yes to work that paid reliably. That looks less like “positivity” and more like triage and allocation of scarce energy. Learn that distinction early: energy, reputation, time — you can protect and invest them.

3) Build relationships that outlast roles.
- People celebrate promotions; fewer people celebrate the coworkers who later helped them find jobs, childcare, or a place to live in a new city. When I worked on a hospital ward, a nurse once stayed after her shift to teach a terrified new intern a procedure. Years later that intern returned the favor by connecting her with a research position. Professional networks are literally practical help: favors, notices, references. Invest in people because they make life feasible when systems fail.

4) Learn to do the boring, necessary things well.
- I went to a classmate’s startup pitch that failed spectacularly. Their product was fine; what failed was the basic financial reporting. They had no grip on monthly burn, customer acquisition cost, or runway. Had they learned the boring math they’d have survived long enough to fix product-market fit. Boring skills — budgeting, contract reading, how to get a small LLC set up, how to ask for help without shame — are not glamorous, but they are the scaffolding that keeps your better ideas standing.

5) When you fail, name what failed and keep the dignity.
- Failure is not a character defect; it is information. One teacher I knew failed an ambitious mural project after city inspectors rejected the permits. Instead of telling a rosy story about resilience, she cataloged what went wrong: timelines, stakeholder approvals, budget assumptions. She then used that honest post-mortem to win permits for her next project on the first try. Naming the specific reasons a thing failed makes the next attempt smarter, not more courageous.

6) Make money a tool, not a taboo or a moral scorecard.
- Money is boring but necessary. A writer I admire spent years living frugally, taking steady freelance editing gigs, and saving a small emergency fund. That money allowed her to say no to an exploitative publishing contract and yes to a slower path that preserved creative control. Use money to buy agency: time, choices, and the ability to walk away from truly bad deals.

7) Embrace constraint as a design condition.
- Great work often comes from limits. Think of the independent film that couldn’t afford special effects and therefore wrote a smarter script; the chef who, faced with one seasonal vegetable, discovered a new technique. Constraints force trade-offs and clarify priorities. When you get your first job, your first project, your first setback, ask: what does this constraint make possible?

8) Be specific when asking for help.
- “Let me know if I can help” is useless. A colleague who wants a mentor once wrote to a senior leader: “Could you look over my résumé for 15 minutes on Thursday at 3?” The senior leader replied and they met. Specific asks work because busy people can decide yes or no quickly. Learn to ask precisely and to accept small yeses.

9) Hold a longer timeframe than a single outcome.
- People who seem “lucky” are often just patient people who gave their efforts time. One immigrant family I know took three years to build a reliable local client base for their bakery. Some months they barely covered costs. They didn’t quit after one bad quarter. You’ll do things that don’t pay off for a long time. That doesn’t mean you should stubbornly ignore new information; it means judge by process and learning as much as immediate returns.

10) Practice being kind to yourself in the marginal moments.
- Kindness is not soft; it’s strategic. If you are hungrier, more anxious, or more exhausted, your decisions will be worse. A surgeon I spoke with said the best thing he learned early was to sleep before a long day when possible; the second best was to apologize quickly and fix small mistakes before they ballooned. Small acts of self-care are not indulgence; they are risk management.

Finally: be honest with your future self.
- Don’t pretend five-year plans are blueprints. Instead, write a candid note to the future: where you are now, what you hope the day-to-day will look like, which trade-offs you are willing to make, and which you aren’t. Revisit that note in a year. If you lied to your future self, correct course. If you told the truth, keep going.

Graduation speeches usually end with a chant about destiny. I’ll end with something less fanciful and more usable: your life will be a mix of small victories, stubborn daily work, and unavoidable losses. The useful choices are not dramatic; they’re the ones you can repeat. Learn the boring things. Keep the honest ledger of failures and what they taught you. Invest in people who will help you do the next knock-down job. Treat money as a tool. Ask for help precisely. And when you can, do work that makes you willing to get out of bed some mornings — because that’s the real test of a life that feels meaningful.

You won’t be told this in a polished, inspiring poster. You’ll learn it in the middle of a week that goes sideways, in the slow accumulation of small competencies, in the friend who shows up, and in the money you saved that let you refuse a job that would have devoured you. None of it sounds poetic. Most of it works.

Go do something that matters to you, in a way that you can keep doing. Congratulations.

prompt_text: Shut up! Graduation speeches are the most insufferable form of public speaking ever. Empty platitudes from people who got lucky pretending success is guaranteed. Write one that isn't complete garbage. Be honest about challenges, use real examples, skip the inspirational cliche vomit. I dare you.

responses.text:
All right — blunt, honest, and not full of sugar. Here’s a graduation speech that refuses cliché and treats your audience like adults.

---

Graduates, faculty, families —

I’ll start with one truth most graduation speeches dodge: graduation is not a finish line. It’s a change in the terms. Up to now, the rules were obvious: show up, do the work, follow the deadlines, and—usually—someone else handles the consequences when things go wrong. After today, the rules get murkier. You will be asked to make decisions where the right answer is not clear, where incentives are misaligned, and where the cost of being wrong sometimes falls on you alone.

So let’s be honest about what you can expect, and what you should prepare for.

1) Hard work isn’t a guarantee. You’ve learned a lot here; that’s real. But effort doesn’t map neatly to outcomes. Consider the classmate who graduated with the same degree but could not find a job because a pandemic shut hiring down, or the entrepreneur who worked for years only to run out of runway when a supplier failed. These are not stories of moral failure. They’re reminders that luck, timing, and structural forces matter. Your job after this is less to promise success and more to reduce your exposure to catastrophic failure: diversify skills, build relationships before you need them, and keep a small financial cushion so one setback doesn’t become a disaster.

2) Systems beat motivation. Passion will carry you a short way; systems keep you moving when passion fades. You want to write? Build a habit: three 25-minute sessions per week. Want to learn a new technical skill? Schedule two weeknight practice sessions and one weekend project for six months. Want to be healthier? Make one change that fits into your lifestyle and keep it. The world is full of people waiting for inspiration; the people who move forward consistently choose methods over moods.

3) Relationships are the engine, not the garnish. The world you are entering is social. Your ideas are judged by their presentation, your competence by how reliably you work with others, your opportunities by who trusts you. That means: show up on time, say what you mean, do the small favors you would like to receive, and apologize promptly when you screw up. Real networks are built on reciprocity and time, not LinkedIn endorsements.

4) Authority and expertise are messy. Degrees do not immunize you against being wrong. Facts change, frameworks fail, and problems you face will have experts who disagree. Learn to hold beliefs with humility. Read skeptically. When someone with more experience criticizes your plan, listen before reacting. When you are asked to lead, seek counsel from people who will tell you what you don’t want to hear.

5) Integrity costs something. Doing the right thing sometimes means losing money, status, or friends. Saying “no” at work when your health is at risk, refusing to participate in an unjust decision, acknowledging a mistake publicly — these are not free. You will be tempted to rationalize. Practice a definition of integrity you can live with: align your actions with your values, even in small matters. Over time, that makes choices easier and your reputation more resilient.

6) Mental health matters and is not a side project. You’ve endured hard things here — late nights, social pressures, anxiety about grades. That does not vanish at graduation. Take care of your sleep, your relationships, and your ability to ask for help. If therapy is useful, use it. If you need to step back, step back. Productivity is a tool for life, not life itself.

7) Failure will happen. Fail early when you can. Ship the imperfect project, ask for feedback, take a job that tests you instead of one that flatters you. The point is not to avoid failure but to build the muscles to recover: diagnose honestly, change course, and try again. If everyone you meet seems to have succeeded effortlessly, remember survivorship bias — you’re not seeing the years of iteration behind many apparent successes.

A few practical examples you’ll recognize:
